pages.title: Duality
pages.html:
5 Duality

This presentation contains some theorems and compatibilities, all relating to the duality of Poincaré. 1, the theorem of local bidality in dimension \(1\) [21] and a few calculations of two. 2, a very economical demonstration of the duality of Poincaré on the curves, which Mr. Artin taught me. 3, a compatibility which makes the link between two definitions of mating which gives rise to the duality of Poincaré for curves: by cup-product, or by self-duality of the jacobian. 4Finally, proof of the compatibility of the title.

Throughout the presentation, the diagrams will be noetherian and separate, and \(n\) is an invertible integer on all the schematics considered.

pages.title: Local Bidality, in dimension \(1\)
pages.html:
1 Local Bidality, Size \(1\)

1.1

Either \(S\) a regular scheme of purely size \(1\). We propose to show that the complex reduced to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) by degree \(0\) is dualistic, i.e. only for \(\mathscr{K}\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_c^b(S,\mathbb{Z}/n)\) (\((-)_c\) for constructible), if installed \(D\mathscr{K}=\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathscr{K},\mathbb{Z}/n)\), then \(D\mathscr{K}\) is still constructible to bounded cohomology, and that canonical morphism \(\alpha\) of the \(\mathscr{K}\) in \(D D\mathscr{K}\) is isomorphism.

For \(\mathscr{K}\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-\), and \(\mathscr{L}\)

\[ \hom(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{L},\mathbb{Z}/n) = \hom(\mathscr{L},\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{K},\mathbb{Z}/n)) \text{.} \]

The morphism of \(\mathscr{K}\) in \(DD\mathscr{K}\) is defined by assuming \(D\mathscr{K}\) in \(\mathsf{D}^-\); if \(\beta:D\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\) is the canonical coupling, it is defined by the coupling \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} D\mathscr{K}=D\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{K} \xrightarrow{\beta} \mathbb{Z}/n\).

1.2

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of the finite type. Possions \(\mathscr{K}_X=\mathsf{R} f^!\mathbb{Z}/n\). For \(\mathscr{K}\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}^-(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\), we place \(D\mathscr{K} = \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{K}_X)\). The addition between \(\mathsf{R} f_!\) and \(\mathsf{R} f^!\) ensures that

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R} f_\ast \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R} f_\ast D\mathscr{K} \ar[r] & \mathsf{R} f_! (\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} D\mathscr{K}) \ar[r] & \mathsf{R} f_! \mathsf{R} f^! \mathbb{Z}/n \ar[r] & \mathbb{Z}/n . } \]

The symmetry of this description shows that for \(f\) own diagram

\[ \tag{1.2.1} \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R} f_\ast \mathscr{K} \ar[r] \ar[d] & D D \mathsf{R} f_\ast \mathscr{K} \ar[d]^-\wr \\ \mathsf{R} f_\ast D D \mathscr{K} \ar[r]^\sim & D\mathsf{R} f_\ast D\mathscr{K} } \]

is commutative.

If \(f\) is the inclusion of a closed point, we know that \(\mathsf{R} f^!\mathbb{Z}/n=\mathbb{Z}/n(-1)[-2]\) – that is, with torsion and offset, \(\mathbb{Z}/n\). On the spectrum of a field, this complex is dualistic (Pontrajagin duality for \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules).1.2.1), we have \(\mathscr{K}\xrightarrow{\sim} D D\mathscr{K}\) for \(\mathscr{K}\) of the form \(\mathsf{R} f_!\mathscr{L}\) – and therefore when the support of \(\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^\bullet(\mathscr{K})\) is finished.

Theorem 1.3

Be \(j:U\hookrightarrow S\) a dense open \(S\) and \(\mathscr{F}\) a locally constant sheaf constructible \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules on \(U\). We have \(\mathsf{D} j_\ast \mathscr{F} = j_\ast D \mathscr{F}\), i.e. \(\operatorname{Hom}(j_\ast \mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/n) = j_\ast\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/n)\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(j_\ast\mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/n) = 0\) for \(i>0\).

On \(U\), \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(j_\ast\mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/n)=0\) for \(i>0\), because \(\mathscr{F}\) is locally constant (and \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-injective module), while for \(i=0\) It's the dual. \(\mathscr{F}^\vee\) of the \(\mathscr{F}\). We're checking that \(\hom(j_\ast\mathscr{F},\mathbb{Z}/n)=j_\ast\mathscr{F}^\vee\), and it remains to verify the nullity of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) (\(i>0\)) in the points of \(S\setminus U\). The problem is local in these points. This leads us to assume that \(S\) is a strictly local trait and, \(U\) is reduced to its generic point \(\eta\). Either \(I=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\eta}/\eta)\). The sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) identifies with the Galoisian module \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}\), and the special fiber of \(j_\ast \mathscr{F}\) to \(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\).

Either \(i\) the inclusion of the closed point \(s\), and apply \(D\) to the exact follow-up

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & j_! \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & j_\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & i_\ast \mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I \ar[r] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & j_! \mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I \ar[r] \ar[u] & \mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I \ar[r] \ar[u] & i_\ast \mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I \ar[r] \ar[u] & 0 . } \]

We get a triangle morphism

\[ \xymatrix{ i_\ast\left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee (-1)[-2] \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & D j_\ast\mathscr{F} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}^\vee \ar[d] \\ i_\ast\left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee(-1)[-2] \ar[r] & \left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee \ar[r] & \mathsf{R} j_\ast \mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I . } \]

The exact long sequence deduced from the first line provides the nullity of \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) (\(i>2\)) and, taking the fiber in \(s\), we find

\[ \xymatrix@=0.7cm{ 0 \ar[r] & \left(\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^1(D j_\ast\mathscr{F}\right)_s \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(I,\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^\vee\right) \ar[r]^-\partial \ar[d] & \left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee(-1) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \left(\underline{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^2 D j_\ast\mathscr{F}\right)_s \ar[r] & 0 \\ & 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(I,(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I)^\vee\right) \ar[r]^-\partial & \left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee(-1) \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Since \(\left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^I\right)^\vee = \left(\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^\vee\right)_I\), posing \(M=\mathscr{F}_{\overline{\eta}}^\vee\), it is finally necessary to check that

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(I,M) \ar[r]^-\sim & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(I,M_I) . } \]

If \(p\) is the residual characteristic exponent, \(I\) is the extension of an isomorphic group to \(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}_{p'} = \varprojlim_{(m,p)=1} \mathbb{Z}/m\) by \(p\)-group \(P\). Since \(P\) is the first to the order of \(M\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(P,M)=0\) (\(i>0\)) and \((M_I)^P=(M^P)_I\). This allows us to replace \(M\) by \(M^P\) and \(I\) by \(I/P\). Finally, we have a functional isomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}_{p'},M)\sim (\)coinvariants of \(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}_{p'}\), in \(M)\), hence the theory.

Theorem 1.4

For \(\mathscr{K}\in \operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_c^b(S,\mathbb{Z}/n)\), we have \(\mathscr{K}\xrightarrow{\sim} D D \mathscr{K}\).

By unscrewing, it is assumed that \(\mathscr{K}\) is reduced to a constructible sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) in degree \(0\), and that \(\mathscr{F}\) is either finished support (1.2), or of the form \(j_\ast\mathscr{F}_1\) as in 1.3. In this second case, 1.3 brings us back to local bidality for \(\mathscr{F}\) locally constant on \(U\).

pages.title: Poincaré's duality for curves, according to Mr. Artin
pages.html:
2 Poincaré's duality for curves, according to Mr. Artin

2.1

Either \(X\) a projective and smooth curve on \(k\) algebraically closed. \(\mathscr{K}_X=\mathbb{Z}/n(1)[2]\) and, for \(\mathscr{K}\in\operatorname{Ob} \mathsf{D}_c^b(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\), \(D\mathscr{K}=\mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{K}_X)\). For \(M\) a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-module is also laid \(D M = \hom(M,\mathbb{Z}/n)\); similarly for modules complexes. The trace morphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{K}_X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\), or \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,\mathscr{K}_X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\), defines a coupling \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,D\mathscr{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\). The duality of Poincare between cohomology and cohomology with clean supports of an open \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) de \(X\) says that, for \(\mathscr{K}=j_!\mathbb{Z}/n\), this coupling identifies each factor at the dual of the other.

I present below a demonstration, which was communicated to me by Mr. Artin, that for \(\mathscr{K}\in\operatorname{Ob}\mathsf{D}_c^b(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\), this mating is always perfect, i.e. defines an isomorphism

\[ \tag{2.1.1} \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,\mathscr{K}) \ar[r]^-\sim & D \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,D\mathscr{K}) . } \]

For any constructible sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\), posions

\[ '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \mathbb{Z}/n \text{-dual of } \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{-i}(X,D\mathscr{F}) \text{.} \]

That (2.1.1) or isomorphism is equivalent to

Theorem 2.2

For \(\mathscr{F}\) a constructible sheaf of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-modules, we have

\[ \tag{2.2.1} \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r]^-\sim & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) . } \]
Lemma 2.3

Either \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a generically spreading morphism between smooth curves on \(k\). We have \(\mathscr{K}_X=f^\ast\mathscr{K}_Y=\mathsf{R} f^!\mathscr{K}_Y\), with \(\operatorname{tr}_f\) for addition arrow \(\mathsf{R} f_!\mathsf{R} f^!\mathscr{K}_Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{K}_Y\).

We just check that for \(f\) finished, we have \(f_\ast \mathsf{R} f^!\mathscr{K}_Y = \mathsf{R}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n,\mathscr{K}_Y)\) is \(f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n\), with \(\operatorname{tr}_f\) for addition arrow. The nullity of \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) (\(i>0\)) results from 1.3, and the assertion results.

It can be said, more explicitly, that \(\mathsf{R} f^!\) is the searcher derived from the searcher \(f^!:f^!\mathscr{F}(U)=\hom(f_!\mathbb{Z}_U,\mathscr{F})\) and 1.3 implies the nullity of \(\mathsf{R}^i f^!\mathbb{Z}/n\) for \(i>0\).

Corollary 2.4

Be \(f:X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a generically spreading morphism of projective and smooth curves on \(k\). We have \('\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,f_\ast \mathscr{F})='\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathscr{F})\); this isomorphism is functional and the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,f_\ast\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,f_\ast\mathscr{F}) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathscr{F}) } \]

is commutative.

Isomorphism is given by 1.2: \(D f_\ast\mathscr{F}=f_\ast D\mathscr{F}\).

2.5

Prove 2.2. If \(\mathscr{F}\) is reduced to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) in one point, extended by \(0\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(D\mathscr{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^\bullet(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{K}_X)=\mathbb{Z}/n\) in degree \(0\), and in mating \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\mathscr{F})\otimes\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(D\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Z}/n\), we have \(1\otimes 1\mapsto 1\) ([IV]Cycle, 2.1.5): Duality is perfect. The case where \(\mathscr{F}\) is finished is treated the same.

For \(\mathscr{F}\) constructible any, \(\mathscr{A}\) the subbeam of its finished support sections, and \(\mathscr{G}=\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{A}\), the exact continuation

\[ \tag{2.5.1} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{A} \ar[r] & \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & \mathscr{G} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

provides a diagram

\[ \tag{2.5.2} \xymatrix@=.5cm{ & & 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{A}) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-\wr & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{-1}(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{-1}(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{A}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

and compatible isomorphisms \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F})\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{G})\), \('\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{G})\) (\(i\ne 0,-1\)). If \(j:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is an open on which \(\mathscr{F}\) is locally constant, we have an exact sequence

\[ \tag{2.5.3} \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{G} \ar[r] & j_\ast j^\ast \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & \mathscr{B} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

with \(\mathscr{B}\) with finished support. Since \('\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,j_\ast j^\ast\mathscr{F}) = D \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2-i} (X,j_\ast(j^\ast\mathscr{F})^\vee(1))\) according to 1.3, this \('\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i=0\) for \(i<0\) and (2.5.2) and the exact long sequence defined by (2.5.3) show that \('\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i\) is still void for \(i<0\).

If \(\mathscr{F}=\mathbb{Z}/n\), the theorem is true for \(i=0\) and \(2\). This expresses that for \(X\) related trace morphism: \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbb{Z}/n\) is an isomorphism. 2.4, the theorem remains true for \(i=0\) and \(\mathscr{F}=f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n\).

For \(\mathscr{F}\) again any and \(\mathscr{G}\) as above, there are \(f:X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) as in 2.4 such that \(\mathscr{G}\) is immersed in \(f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n\):

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{G} \ar[r] & f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n \ar[r] & \mathscr{Q} \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

Changing \(X'\) into \(X''/X'\), it can be assumed that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,f_\ast\mathbb{Z}/n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X',\mathbb{Z}/n)\) is null for \(i>0\). \[ \xymatrix@=.3cm{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(S',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-\wr & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{Q}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r]^-0 \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{Q}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \cdots \\ 0 \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{Q}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{G}) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & '\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{Q}) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

3 Poincaré Duality for Curves

In this paragraph we prove compatibility ([I]Arcata 6.2.2.3).

3.1

The ratings are those of ([I]Arcata 6.2.3): \(\overline{X}\) is a projective, smooth and connected curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(D\) is a reduced divisor on \(\overline{X}\), \(0\) a point of \(X=\overline{X}\setminus D\)

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j & \overline{X} & D , \ar[l]_-i} \]

Note again \(j\) the inclusion of \(X\times X\) in \(X\times \overline{X}\). \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{A} \ar[r] & \mathscr{B} \ar[r] & \mathscr{C} \ar[r] & 0 . } \]

So we have \(\overline{e} = \partial f_0\), where \(\partial \) is defined by (3.2.2), and where the usual isomorphisms are used \(\mathsf{H}^2(X,\mathscr{F}[-1]) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\).     \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

Proposition 3.4

\(\operatorname{tr}(\varphi(u)\smallsmile x) = \varphi(x)\).

\[ \tag{3.4.1} \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = x \]

\begin{align*} &\xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[l]_-\sim} \\ &\xymatrix{ \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X\times \overline{X},\mathbb{Z}/n\boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) . } \end{align*}

Given the relationship between Künneth and cup-product, we have

\[ \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = -\operatorname{tr}(u'\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) \text{.} \]

The second cup-product is that of ([IV]Cycle 1.2.4):

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X\times \overline{X},\mathbb{Z}/n\boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X}\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\boxtimes \mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^3(\overline{X}\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n \boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) . } \]

The sign – comes from the permutation of two degree symbols \(1\) in (1). Since \(c\smallsmile\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x\) and \(c^{1,1}\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast\) have even \((2,1)\)-component of Künneth, and that \(u'=c^{1,1}\), we have

\[ \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = \operatorname{tr}(c\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) \text{.} \]

\[ \operatorname{tr}(c\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) = \operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{cl}(\Delta)\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) = \operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{cl}(\Delta)\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_2^\ast x) = \operatorname{tr}(\operatorname{cl}(\Delta))\smallsmile x = x \text{.} \]

blocks.html: 3 Poincaré Duality for Curves
In this paragraph we prove compatibility ([I]Arcata 6.2.2.3).

The ratings are those of ([I]Arcata 6.2.3): \(\overline{X}\) is a projective, smooth and connected curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(D\) is a reduced divisor on \(\overline{X}\), \(0\) a point of \(X=\overline{X}\setminus D\)
\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^-j & \overline{X} & D , \ar[l]_-i} \]

and \(\operatorname{Pic}_D(\overline{X})=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X},_D\mathbb{G}_m)\) where \(_D\mathbb{G}_m\) is the sheaf of the sections of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) Congruents to \(1\mod D\)It is recalled that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n\), and that \(x\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}(x)\) defines a canonical application \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}_D(\overline{X})\)We're laying down. \(f_0(x)=f(x)-f(0):X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})\).

Note again \(j\) the inclusion of \(X\times X\) in \(X\times \overline{X}\). The diagonal \(\Delta\) of \(X\times X\) is closed in \(X\times \overline{X}\); it defines a class in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_\Delta^2(X\times X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)=\underline{\operatorname{H}}_\Delta^2(X\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\). Note \(c\) its image in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\).
Künneth's formula ensures that

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(\overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \text{.} \]
We propose to calculate the \((1,1)\)-component of \(c\),

\[ c^{1,1} \in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1\left(X,\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n\right) \text{.} \]
The exact follow-up
\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n \ar[r] & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r]^n & \operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X}) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

made of \(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})\) a \(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n\)-torseur on \(\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})\). Note, \(u\) the class, in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n))=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\operatorname{Pic}_D^0(\overline{X})_n)\), of its reciprocal image by \(f_0\).

With previous notations, \(c^{1,1}=-u\).

1. \(e\in \mathsf{H}^2(X,\mathscr{A})\) comes from \(\widetilde e\in \mathsf{H}^2(X,\tau_{\leqslant 1}\mathscr{A})\) (because its image in \(E^{02}\) is null). Proposition 3.4

\(\operatorname{tr}(\varphi(u)\smallsmile x) = \varphi(x)\).
This identity is deduced, by application of \(\varphi\),
\[ \tag{3.4.1} \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = x \]

so that \(x\mapsto \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x)\) is the compound application

where (1) is given by \(a\otimes b\otimes c\mapsto (a\smallsmile c)\otimes b\).

Let's express this morphism in terms of trace morphism \(\operatorname{tr}:\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^3(X\times X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n^{\otimes 2}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) relative to the second projection. \begin{align*} &\xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)) & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n) \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/n)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[l]_-\sim} \\ &\xymatrix{ \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X\times \overline{X},\mathbb{Z}/n\boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) . } \end{align*}
Given the relationship between Künneth and cup-product, we have
\[ \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = -\operatorname{tr}(u'\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) \text{.} \]
The second cup-product is that of ([IV]Cycle 1.2.4):

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X\times \overline{X},\mathbb{Z}/n\boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(\overline{X}\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\boxtimes \mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^3(\overline{X}\times \overline{X},j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n \boxtimes j_!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) . } \]

The sign – comes from the permutation of two degree symbols \(1\) in (1). Since \(c\smallsmile\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x\) and \(c^{1,1}\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast\) have even \((2,1)\)-component of Künneth, and that \(u'=c^{1,1}\), we have
\[ \operatorname{tr}(u\smallsmile x) = \operatorname{tr}(c\smallsmile \mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x) \text{.} \]

The cup-product on the right can this time be interpreted as the cup-product ([IV]Cycle 1.2.5) of \(\operatorname{cl}(\Delta)\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}_\Delta^2(X\times X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) by \(\mathrm{pr}_1^\ast x\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^1(\Delta,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\). In [2], in the midst of discouragement, I did not check the compatibility of the title. Although it turns out to be smoke-free, I think I have to fix this gap here. For \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) separated of finite type, it was to verify that the addition morphism \(\mathsf{R} f_!\mathsf{R} f^!\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{L}\) is compatible with any live location \(k:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Designated by \(k\) and \(f\) several arrows, as below, it is a question of checking a commutativity:

\[ \xymatrix{ X_V \ar[r]^-k \ar[d]^-f & X \ar[d]^-f \\ V \ar[r]^-k & S } \qquad \xymatrix{ k^\ast \mathsf{R} f_! \mathsf{R} f^!\mathscr{L} \ar[r]^-{k^\ast(\text{Adj})} \ar[d]^-\sim & k^\ast \mathscr{L} \ar@{=}[d] \\ \mathsf{R} f_! \mathsf{R} f^! k^\ast \mathscr{L} \ar[r]^-{\text{Adj}} & k^\ast \mathscr{L} . } \]

At the level of complexes, the desired commutativity is written (loc. cit.)

\[ \xymatrix{ k^\ast f_!^\bullet f^!_\bullet \mathscr{L} \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{(1)} & k^\ast \mathscr{L} \ar@{=}[d] \\ f_!^\bullet f^!_\bullet k^\ast \mathscr{L} \ar[r] & k^\ast \mathscr{L} . } \]

It is the same to deduct \((3)\) from \((2)\) by adding \(f_!\) and \(f^!:k^\ast \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^! f_! k^\ast f^!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^! k^\ast f_! f^! \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^! k^\ast\).

Writing \(k\) for \(k^\ast\) and omitting \(\mathscr{L}\), the commutativity is then that of the outer edge of

\[ \xymatrix@=.5cm{ k f_! f^! \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & f_! k f^! \ar[r] & f_! f^! f_! k f^! \ar[r] & f_! f^! k f_! f^! \ar@{=}[d] \\ k f_! f^! \ar[d] & & & f_! f^! k f_! f^! \ar[d] \ar[lll]\\ k & & & f_! f^! k . \ar[lll]} \]

In the first line, we find a homomorphism \(k _! f^! \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_! f^! k f_! f^!\), defined because \(k f_! f^!\) is of the form \(f_! X\) (\(X=k f^!\)); it admits for retraction the morphism of addition in the second line, and the commutativity of the lower square the functionality of this morphism of addition.
